questions.question: Explain the Shell's Interpretive Cycle with its four main stages.
keyPoints: The cycle is also called REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)
Read stage: Shell reads command input from stdin (keyboard/file)
Evaluate/Parse stage: Shell parses command, performs expansion and substitution
Print stage: Shell executes command and prints output to stdout
Loop stage: Cycle repeats for next command input

questions.question: Differentiate between shell variables and environment variables in Unix shell.
keyPoints: Shell variables are local to current shell session only
Environment variables are exported and inherited by child processes
Shell variables: created using var=value syntax
Environment variables: created using export var=value or export var
Use 'env' or 'printenv' to see environment variables
Use 'set' to see all variables including shell variables

questions.question: Describe the order of shell initialization files executed in different shell types.
keyPoints: Login shell reads: /etc/profile → ~/.bash_profile → ~/.bash_login → ~/.profile (first found)
Non-login interactive shell reads: ~/.bashrc
Non-interactive shell: reads file specified by BASH_ENV or runs in script mode
Login shell executes once at login, non-login for each new terminal

questions.question: Explain job control commands in shell with examples.
keyPoints: jobs: Lists all background/suspended jobs in current shell
fg: Brings background job to foreground (fg %1)
bg: Resumes suspended job in background (bg %1)
Ctrl+Z: Suspends foreground job (sends SIGTSTP)
Ctrl+C: Terminates foreground job (sends SIGINT)
&: Runs command in background (command &)

questions.question: What are shell metacharacters? List and explain any four with examples.
keyPoints: Metacharacters have special meaning to shell, not treated as ordinary characters
* (asterisk): Matches zero or more characters (ls *.txt)
? (question mark): Matches exactly one character (ls file?.txt)
| (pipe): Connects output of one command to input of another (ls | grep txt)
> and <: Redirection operators (cat < input.txt > output.txt)
;: Command separator (cmd1; cmd2) runs sequentially
&: Run in background (sleep 10 &)

questions.question: Explain command substitution and variable expansion in shell with examples.
keyPoints: Command substitution: Output of command used as argument to another
Syntax: $(command) or `command` (backticks)
Example: echo $(date) or echo `date` prints current date
Variable expansion: $var replaces with variable's value
Example: echo $PATH displays PATH variable value
${var} syntax for explicit variable name boundary

questions.question: What is signal handling in shell? Explain with reference to common signals.
keyPoints: Signals are software interrupts sent to processes
SIGINT (Ctrl+C): Interrupt signal, terminates running process by default
SIGTSTP (Ctrl+Z): Stop signal, suspends process to background
SIGKILL: Kill signal, terminates process immediately (cannot be caught)
Shell handles signals during command execution
Shell can ignore, default, or catch signals using trap

questions.question: Explain in detail the various stages of command processing in the shell's interpretive cycle.
keyPoints: 1. Reading: Shell reads command line from stdin using getline() or readline()
2. Tokenization: Breaking input into tokens (words, operators, arguments)
3. Parsing: Analyzing tokens for syntax, building command structure
4. Command Lookup: Searching for command in PATH or as built-in
5. Expansion: Performing variable, command, and filename expansions
6. Redirection Setup: Opening/creating files for stdin/stdout/stderr redirection
7. Pipeline Setup: Creating pipes between commands if present
8. Execution: Using fork() and exec() system calls to run commands
9. Job Control: Managing foreground/background processes
10. Output: Displaying results to stdout
11. Return to read stage for next command

questions.question: Describe the working of input/output redirection and piping in Unix shell with examples.
keyPoints: Redirection changes standard streams: stdin (0), stdout (1), stderr (2)
Output redirection: > overwrites, >> appends (ls > out.txt, ls >> out.txt)
Input redirection: < takes input from file (sort < data.txt)
Error redirection: 2> for stderr, 2>> for append (command 2> errors.txt)
Combined: command > out.txt 2>&1 redirects both to same file
Piping: | connects stdout of left command to stdin of right
Examples: cat file.txt | grep 'pattern' | sort | uniq
Multiple pipes create data processing pipeline

questions.question: Explain the difference between login shell and non-login shell. Discuss their initialization process.
keyPoints: Login shell: First shell after user login, used for system authentication
Non-login shell: Subsequent shells opened in terminal emulator
Login shell reads initialization files in order: /etc/profile, ~/.bash_profile, ~/.bash_login, ~/.profile
Non-login shell reads only ~/.bashrc
Login shell is interactive; non-login can be interactive or non-interactive (script)
Environment variables set in login shell are inherited by child shells
Typical setup: ~/.bash_profile sources ~/.bashrc for consistent environment
Use 'echo $-' to check shell options, 'shopt login_shell' to check login status

questions.question: Discuss process creation and execution mechanism in Unix shell using fork() and exec() system calls.
keyPoints: Shell uses fork() and exec() for command execution
fork(): Creates a child process that is a copy of parent
Parent process gets child's PID, child gets 0 as return value
After fork(), either parent waits (wait) or childexecutes new program
exec() family replaces current process image with new program
execvp() searches PATH for executable, execle() uses explicit environment
Shell does: fork() → child does execvp() → parent does wait() for child
This creates process hierarchy with shell as parent, commands as children
Background jobs: parent doesn't wait, continues immediately for next command
